parsed.message:
Your geyser appears to have a corroded internal tank, which is causing the rusty brown water in the drip tray and the visible leak. This internal corrosion is also likely responsible for the increased electricity bills and reduced hot water duration you have noticed.

Geyser tanks corrode when the internal sacrificial anode, designed to protect the tank walls, has been fully consumed. Once the anode is gone, the tank's steel walls begin to rust, leading to leaks and compromised thermal efficiency.

If left unaddressed, the corrosion will worsen, leading to more significant leaks and potentially a catastrophic burst of the geyser tank. This could cause extensive water damage to your property.

There is a risk of electrical shorting if water comes into contact with the geyser's electrical components. It is important to switch off the geyser's power at the distribution board to prevent this hazard.

This close-up view focuses on the copper pipe connections leading into the geyser, showing heavy corrosion and white mineral deposits around the brass fittings and the base of the copper pipe. The pipe itself appears discoloured and worn.

This image provides an external, wider view of the building, showing an outdoor walkway with brick walls and windows. It offers context of the property's exterior but no direct diagnostic information related to the geyser.

parsed.diagnosis: Leaking Geyser Inlet or Outlet Fitting
parsed.estimated_diagnosis_sentence: Leaking Geyser Inlet or Outlet Fitting
parsed.message:
Your geyser appears to have a leak from one of its inlet or outlet fittings. The first image clearly shows water dripping from a copper pipe connection and collecting in the drip tray below the geyser. This indicates that a joint or connection point on the geyser's pipework has failed.

This type of leak often develops due to thermal cycling, which can cause compression nuts or flexible connectors to loosen over time. Alternatively, flexible braided connectors have a finite lifespan, typically 5-7 years, after which they can start to weep or fail.

If left unaddressed, this continuous leak will lead to ongoing water wastage and could cause significant water damage to the surrounding structure, including ceilings, walls, and flooring. The constant presence of moisture can also encourage mould growth and compromise the integrity of building materials.
image_descriptions: This image shows a close-up of a copper pipe connection with visible water droplets actively dripping from a brass fitting. A black plastic drip tray below contains a pool of rusty, discoloured water, indicating an ongoing leak from the geyser system.

This image provides a different angle of pipework entering a wall, showing white plastic pipes alongside a copper pipe. There is visible discolouration and some white mineral deposits around the entry point of the copper pipe, suggesting a history of water leakage in this area.

This image presents an exterior view of an apartment building with multiple windows and a brick wall. It offers environmental context but does not show any specific plumbing equipment or direct evidence of a fault.
image_thought_breakdown: This image shows a close-up of a copper pipe connection with visible water droplets actively dripping from a brass fitting. A black plastic drip tray below contains a pool of rusty, discoloured water, indicating an ongoing leak from the geyser system.

This image provides a different angle of pipework entering a wall, showing white plastic pipes alongside a copper pipe. There is visible discolouration and some white mineral deposits around the entry point of the copper pipe, suggesting a history of water leakage in this area.

This image presents an exterior view of an apartment building with multiple windows and a brick wall. It offers environmental context but does not show any specific plumbing equipment or direct evidence of a fault.
contractor_checklist: Isolate the water supply to the geyser and drain the affected line.
Inspect the cold inlet and hot outlet fittings, including any flexible connectors, for the source of the leak.
Replace the failed compression fitting, flexible connector, or copper joint with new, appropriate plumbing components.
Test the repaired connection under pressure to ensure it is watertight and functioning correctly.
parsed.homeowner_prep: Locate and turn off the main water supply valve to your property, or the isolation valve specifically for the geyser, to minimise further water damage before the plumber arrives.
parsed.diy_verification: Place a dry paper towel directly under the dripping connection; if it becomes wet within a short period, it confirms an active leak from that fitting.
confidence_drivers: Clear visual evidence of water dripping from a pipe connection
Accumulation of rusty water in the drip tray
User's explicit statement of a geyser leak
Visible discolouration and mineral deposits on adjacent pipework

parsed.message:
Your garage door appears to have a missing or broken counterbalance spring on one side. This spring is crucial for balancing the door's weight, allowing the motor to lift it smoothly or for you to open it manually with ease.

Garage door springs are under immense tension and are designed to counterbalance the heavy door. When one breaks or goes missing, the entire weight of the door shifts, causing it to become extremely heavy and often leading to misalignment or the motor struggling to operate it. This sudden imbalance can also cause other components, like the motor's connecting arm, to detach or bend.

Continuing to operate the door with a missing or broken spring can place excessive strain on the garage door opener motor, leading to its premature failure. It can also cause further damage to the door panels, tracks, and rollers due to the uneven load.

Attempting to lift or operate a garage door with a broken or missing spring can be very dangerous. The door is extremely heavy and can fall unexpectedly, causing serious injury. It is best to avoid operating the door until a professional has assessed and repaired it.
image_descriptions: This image shows the left side of the garage door, which is visibly sagging downwards. A black bracket is attached to the door, but the counterbalance spring that should connect it to the frame is absent, leaving a clear gap.

The wider view in this image reveals the entire garage door hanging at an uneven angle, lower on the left side where the spring is missing. The ceiling-mounted motor and its detached connecting arm are visible above the door.
A close-up of the ceiling rail shows the detached motor arm hanging freely, no longer connected to the door. The red emergency release cord is also visible, hanging from the motor unit.

This image provides a detailed view of the attachment point on the garage door where the motor arm should connect. The metal bracket is present on the door, but the arm itself is clearly detached from it.
image_thought_breakdown: This image shows the left side of the garage door, which is visibly sagging downwards. A black bracket is attached to the door, but the counterbalance spring that should connect it to the frame is absent, leaving a clear gap.

The wider view in this image reveals the entire garage door hanging at an uneven angle, lower on the left side where the spring is missing. The ceiling-mounted motor and its detached connecting arm are visible above the door.
A close-up of the ceiling rail shows the detached motor arm hanging freely, no longer connected to the door. The red emergency release cord is also visible, hanging from the motor unit.

This image provides a detailed view of the attachment point on the garage door where the motor arm should connect. The metal bracket is present on the door, but the arm itself is clearly detached from it.
contractor_checklist: Inspect the garage door system to identify the exact type of spring required and assess any secondary damage.
Replace the missing or broken counterbalance spring with a new one matched to the door's weight.
Re-balance the garage door to ensure it operates smoothly and safely.
Re-attach and inspect the motor connecting arm and other hardware for proper function and alignment.
parsed.homeowner_prep: Keep the garage door disengaged from the motor and avoid attempting to open or close it manually until a technician arrives.
parsed.diy_verification: With the motor disengaged, attempt to lift the door manually; if it feels extremely heavy and does not stay balanced, the spring system is compromised.
confidence_drivers: User explicitly stated 'spring is missing on one side'
Image 1 clearly shows the left side sagging and a missing spring mechanism
Image 2 confirms the door's severe misalignment
Detached motor arm is a cascading effect of unbalanced door

parsed.diagnosis: Garage Door Off Track or Misaligned
parsed.estimated_diagnosis_sentence: Garage Door Off Track or Misaligned
parsed.message:
Your garage door appears to be off its track or severely misaligned on the left side. This is evident from the door hanging at an angle and the lifting arm being detached from the door panel.

This issue typically develops when the door encounters an obstruction during its operation, or if the track itself becomes bent or damaged. When a roller comes out of its channel, the door loses its guided path and can jam or fall out of alignment.
image_descriptions: This image shows the left side of the garage door, where the lifting arm is visibly detached from the door panel, causing the door to hang crookedly. The chain link fence and a concrete pillar are also visible on the left.

This wider shot captures the entire garage door from the inside, clearly showing it hanging at a severe angle, lower on the left side. The ceiling-mounted motor and central rail are visible above the door.
A close-up view of the central rail and the emergency release cord, which is hanging down. The attachment point of the motor arm to the door's top bracket is visible, showing it is still connected.

This image provides a detailed view of the central attachment bracket on the top edge of the garage door, where the motor arm connects. The connecting pin and bolts are clearly visible and appear intact in this specific area.

contractor_checklist: Re-seat the garage door rollers back into their tracks.
Inspect the entire track system for any bends, damage, or obstructions and straighten or repair as necessary.
Replace any damaged rollers or lifting cables that may have contributed to the misalignment.
Re-balance the door and test its smooth operation through a full cycle.
parsed.homeowner_prep: Ensure the area around the garage door tracks is clear of any obstructions to allow the technician easy access.
parsed.diy_verification: Carefully inspect both side tracks of the garage door for any rollers that have come out of their channels or any bent sections of the track.

Overall tilt of the garage door visible in wide shot
Motor and central attachment appear intact
Consistent evidence across multiple images

parsed.message:
Your geyser appears to have a corroded tank. The rusty brown water visible in the drip tray and the reported discoloured hot water from your taps are strong indicators that the internal sacrificial anode has been consumed, allowing the steel tank wall to rust through.

This corrosion typically develops over time as the geyser's internal sacrificial anode, designed to protect the tank, gradually depletes. Once the anode is gone, the tank itself begins to corrode, leading to leaks and discoloured water.

If left unaddressed, the corrosion will worsen, increasing the risk of a catastrophic tank burst. This could lead to significant water damage to your property and potentially create an electrical hazard if water comes into contact with wiring.

parsed.diagnosis: Leaking Geyser Inlet or Outlet Fitting
parsed.estimated_diagnosis_sentence: Leaking Geyser Inlet or Outlet Fitting
parsed.message:
Your geyser appears to be leaking from one of its pipe connections or fittings, as evidenced by the water collecting in the drip tray and the visible drips from the brass fitting. The discoloured water in the tray suggests that there might also be some sediment or corrosion present within the system.

This type of leak often develops when compression fittings or flexible connectors on the cold inlet or hot outlet lines begin to weep. Thermal cycling, which is common with geysers, can gradually loosen connections or degrade the seals and materials over time, leading to small, continuous drips.

If left unaddressed, even a small leak can lead to significant water damage to ceilings, walls, or flooring over time. The continuous dripping also wastes water and energy, as the geyser will constantly reheat the lost water. Furthermore, prolonged exposure to moisture can create an environment conducive to mould growth and compromise the structural integrity of surrounding materials.
image_descriptions: This image shows a close-up of a geyser's drip tray containing brown, murky water. Active drips are visible from a brass fitting connecting a copper pipe to a black plastic component, indicating a leak at this specific union.

This image provides a different angle of copper pipework entering a wall, with visible signs of corrosion and staining around the pipe's entry point, suggesting long-term moisture exposure in this area.
This image presents an exterior view of an apartment building with a walkway and windows, offering environmental context but no direct diagnostic information about the geyser issue.
image_thought_breakdown: This image shows a close-up of a geyser's drip tray containing brown, murky water. Active drips are visible from a brass fitting connecting a copper pipe to a black plastic component, indicating a leak at this specific union.

This image provides a different angle of copper pipework entering a wall, with visible signs of corrosion and staining around the pipe's entry point, suggesting long-term moisture exposure in this area.
This image presents an exterior view of an apartment building with a walkway and windows, offering environmental context but no direct diagnostic information about the geyser issue.
contractor_checklist: Isolate the water supply to the geyser and drain the affected line.
Inspect all inlet and outlet fittings, including flexible connectors and the pressure control valve, to pinpoint the exact source of the leak.
Replace any faulty compression fittings, flexible hoses, or valves with new, compliant components.
Test the geyser system thoroughly for leaks and proper operation once repairs are complete.
parsed.homeowner_prep: Turn off the main water supply to your property and switch off the geyser's circuit breaker at your electrical distribution board to prevent further water damage and ensure safety.
parsed.diy_verification: Place a dry sheet of paper or a paper towel directly under the dripping fitting; if it becomes wet within an hour, an active leak is confirmed.
confidence_drivers: User explicitly stated 'My geyser is leaking'
Image 1 shows active drips from a geyser fitting
Image 1 shows discoloured water in the drip tray
Image 2 shows corrosion on pipework

parsed.message:
Your garage door appears to have a missing or broken counterbalance spring on one side. This spring is crucial for balancing the door's weight, allowing the motor to open and close it with minimal effort. When a spring fails, the door becomes significantly heavier on the affected side, putting excessive strain on the opener motor and causing it to stop or beep.

Counterbalance springs, whether torsion or extension types, are under high tension and are designed to assist in lifting the door. They typically fail due to metal fatigue after many years of use, often with a loud snapping sound. Once one spring fails, the remaining spring and the motor are forced to bear the full, unbalanced load, which they are not designed to do.

Rebalance the garage door and test the opener motor for smooth and safe operation.

parsed.message:
Your garage door has a detached lifting arm on one side, causing the door to hang unevenly. This detachment means the door is no longer properly supported and balanced, making it difficult or impossible to operate smoothly.

This issue typically develops when a component in the door's counterbalance system, such as a tension spring or lifting cable, fails or breaks. When the spring or cable can no longer support its share of the door's weight, the entire load shifts, often causing the lifting arm or its connection point to bend, break, or detach under the sudden, excessive strain. It can also occur if the door is forced open or closed against an obstruction.

Operating the door in this condition can cause further damage to the motor, tracks, or other door panels. It could also lead to the door falling unexpectedly, posing a significant safety hazard.

This image offers a closer view of the overhead rail and the emergency release mechanism, showing the red cord and a warning tag. The connection point of the lifting arm to the overhead trolley is visible, but the arm's connection to the door itself is out of frame.

The final image is a close-up of the connection point where the lifting arm attaches to the door's top panel, showing the metal bracket and bolts. The arm itself is not present at this connection, confirming its detachment from the door.

parsed.message:
The rusty brown water pooling in your drip tray, combined with a sudden spike in your electricity bill and a shortage of hot water, strongly suggests that your geyser's inner cylinder has corroded and breached. When the protective sacrificial anode inside the tank is fully depleted, the water begins to corrode the steel cylinder itself, leading to rust-coloured leaks and eventual tank failure.

At the same time, heavy mineral scale has likely coated the heating element. This scale acts as an insulator, forcing the element to run much longer and draw far more electricity to heat the same volume of water, while also reducing the overall hot water capacity.
parsed.action_required:

When the protective sacrificial anode inside the tank is fully depleted, the water begins to corrode the steel cylinder itself, leading to rust-coloured leaks and eventual tank failure.

At the same time, heavy mineral scale has likely coated the heating element. This scale acts as an insulator, forcing the element to run much longer and draw far more electricity to heat the same volume of water, while also reducing the overall hot water capacity.

parsed.message:
The garage door is failing to open because the left-hand side counterbalance tension spring is missing. Without this spring to share the load, the heavy wooden door is severely unbalanced, placing immense strain on the motor.

Counterbalance springs are under high tension and can snap or detach due to metal fatigue over years of operation. When one spring is lost, the garage door opener detects the sudden resistance as an obstruction, triggering a safety reverse and warning beeps to protect the motor from burning out.

Operating the door in this unbalanced state can strip the motor gears, bend the drawbar, or damage the wooden door panel.

Do not attempt to operate the door automatically or stand near the mechanism, as the remaining single spring is under double the normal tension and could fail suddenly.
parsed.action_required:
The garage door is failing to open because the left-hand side counterbalance tension spring is missing. Without this spring to share the load, the heavy wooden door is severely unbalanced, placing immense strain on the motor.

Counterbalance springs are under high tension and can snap or detach due to metal fatigue over years of operation. When one spring is lost, the garage door opener detects the sudden resistance as an obstruction, triggering a safety reverse and warning beeps to protect the motor from burning out.

Operating the door in this unbalanced state can strip the motor gears, bend the drawbar, or damage the wooden door panel.

Do not attempt to operate the door automatically or stand near the mechanism, as the remaining single spring is under double the normal tension and could fail suddenly.

The garage door is hanging unevenly because the left-side tension spring is completely missing, while the right-side spring remains intact. This asymmetry prevents the door from balancing correctly during operation.

Garage door counterbalance springs are under high tension and fatigue over hundreds of cycles, eventually snapping or detaching. When one spring fails, the remaining spring cannot lift the heavy wooden door alone, causing the door to twist and putting severe strain on the opener motor and connecting arm.

Operating the door in this unbalanced state can burn out the opener motor or cause the door to fall suddenly, which is a significant safety hazard.

parsed.message:
The standing rusty water in your drip tray combined with a drop in hot water performance indicates that your geyser's inner steel cylinder has likely corroded and breached. When the protective sacrificial anode inside the tank fully depletes, the water begins to corrode the steel tank itself, leading to structural failure and rust-coloured leaks.

This internal corrosion often compromises the heating element as well. Mineral scale and rust build-up around the element force it to run longer and draw more electricity to heat the water, which explains your rising utility bills and why the hot water is running out so quickly.

Because this involves an active leak and electrical components, the water can eventually damage your ceiling and create a short-circuit hazard if left unaddressed.

This internal corrosion often compromises the heating element as well. Mineral scale and rust build-up around the element force it to run longer and draw more electricity to heat the water, which explains your rising utility bills and why the hot water is running out so quickly.

Because this involves an active leak and electrical components, the water can eventually damage your ceiling and create a short-circuit hazard if left unaddressed.

critique.knowledge_gap: The pipeline did not correctly interpret the confidence threshold for committing to a diagnosis when strong evidence is present.
critique.resolution_would_be: The system needs to correctly apply the rubric to commit to a diagnosis when the user's description and visual evidence are clear and consistent.

confidence_calibration.delta_reasoning: The agent's confidence of 0 is miscalibrated given the strong evidence. The user explicitly states 'The geyser is leaking, the water in the drip tray is rusty brown, our electricity bill has gone up and the hot water doesn't last as long.' The images clearly show a geyser with rusty water in the drip tray and active dripping. This combination of explicit user description and visual evidence strongly supports the diagnosis of a leaking geyser, likely due to internal corrosion, and the associated symptoms of increased electricity usage and reduced hot water. The agent's prose diagnosis was accurate, but the confidence score and `requires_clarification` flag were contradictory and incorrect.

critique.notes_for_human_review: The agent provided a very accurate diagnosis in the prose, correctly identifying a corroded inner geyser cylinder based on the user's description and images. However, it set `confidence` to 0 and `requires_clarification` to true, which directly contradicts the detailed diagnosis provided. The `action_required` field was also 'N/A' despite a clear fault. This indicates a miscalibration in how the confidence and `requires_clarification` flags are set, preventing a commit when one was warranted.

parsed.message:
Your geyser is experiencing an active leak, as evidenced by the rusty water pooling in the drip tray and visible droplets forming on the brass T-piece fitting. This type of leak often points to a failing safety valve or a compromised connection where the pipe meets the cylinder.

Over time, pressure relief valves can fail due to mineral scale build-up or internal spring fatigue, preventing them from sealing completely. If left unaddressed, a minor leak can worsen, leading to an overflowing drip tray, potential ceiling damage, and increased energy bills as the system struggles to maintain temperature.

However, the diagnosis title 'Leaking Geyser Safety Valve' was too specific given the visual evidence and the user's general statement 'My geyser is leaking'. The image shows a leak at a brass T-piece, but it's not definitively a safety valve or a specific cylinder connection without closer inspection or user input. My critique confidence reflects that a leak is clearly present, but the specific component is not identifiable from the provided images.

critique.notes_for_human_review: The agent correctly identified a leak from a geyser and requested clarification, which was the right outcome. However, the diagnosis title was overly specific ('Leaking Geyser Safety Valve') given the ambiguity of the visual evidence. The prompt should encourage more general diagnosis titles when clarification is needed for specific component identification.

parsed.thought: Symmetry check: Comparing the two sides of the garage door reveals a stark asymmetry. The right side has a heavy counterbalance tension spring installed, whereas the left side has an empty bracket with no spring present. This missing spring means the heavy wooden door is completely unbalanced. When the motor attempts to lift it, the excessive weight triggers the opener's safety overload sensor, causing the motor to beep and reverse the door to the closed position.

The right side has a heavy counterbalance tension spring installed, whereas the left side has an empty bracket with no spring present. When the motor attempts to lift it, the excessive weight triggers the opener's safety overload sensor, causing the motor to beep and reverse the door to the closed position.

thoughts_history.thought: Symmetry check: Comparing the two sides of the garage door reveals a stark asymmetry. The right side has a heavy counterbalance tension spring installed, whereas the left side has an empty bracket with no spring present. This missing spring means the heavy wooden door is completely unbalanced. When the motor attempts to lift it, the excessive weight triggers the opener's safety overload sensor, causing the motor to beep and reverse the door to the closed position.

parsed.message:
The garage door is failing to open because the counterbalance tension spring on the left-hand side is completely missing. Tilt-up wooden garage doors rely on a matched pair of heavy-duty springs to offset their substantial weight, allowing the motor to lift them with minimal effort.

When one spring is absent, the motor is forced to lift almost the entire dead weight of the heavy timber door. This extreme load triggers the opener's safety obstruction sensor, which interprets the resistance as an obstacle, stops the travel, beeps, and reverses the door to prevent damage to the motor gears.
parsed.action_required:
The garage door is failing to open because the counterbalance tension spring on the left-hand side is completely missing. Tilt-up wooden garage doors rely on a matched pair of heavy-duty springs to offset their substantial weight, allowing the motor to lift them with minimal effort.

When one spring is absent, the motor is forced to lift almost the entire dead weight of the heavy timber door. This extreme load triggers the opener's safety obstruction sensor, which interprets the resistance as an obstacle, stops the travel, beeps, and reverses the door to prevent damage to the motor gears.
image_descriptions: A close-up of the left-hand side hinge mechanism showing the mounting bracket and pivot arms attached to the wall and wooden door frame, with no tension spring present.

A wide view of the entire wooden tilt-up garage door in a partially open, tilted position, showing the ceiling-mounted motor rail and the asymmetry between the spring-less left side and the spring-equipped right side.
A view looking up at the central ceiling rail, showing the red emergency release cord hanging down and the curved towing arm connected to the top edge of the wooden door.
A close-up of the metal header bracket bolted to the top centre of the wooden door panel, securing the connection pin and cotter pin of the motor's towing arm.

parsed.homeowner_prep: Keep the garage door closed and avoid operating the motor to prevent burning out the drive gear.
parsed.diy_verification: With the motor disengaged via the red cord, attempt to lift the door manually; if it feels extremely heavy and difficult to lift, the counterbalance system is inactive.
confidence_drivers: Clear visual absence of the left tension spring
User description perfectly matches an unbalanced door overload symptom
Right-side spring is clearly visible for comparison

confidence_calibration.delta_reasoning: The agent correctly identified the missing counterbalance tension spring, which is explicitly stated by the user and clearly visible in the images. The diagnosis is specific and accurate, and the explanation of the mechanism (motor overload) is sound. There is no ambiguity or lack of information, yet the agent reported 0 confidence and set `requires_clarification` to true, which is a significant miscalibration.

critique.notes_for_human_review: The agent correctly diagnosed the issue based on clear visual evidence and user input, but failed to commit. It set confidence to 0 and `requires_clarification` to true, which is a clear miscalibration of the rubric. The `action_required` field was also incorrectly set to 'N/A' despite a clear diagnosis.
